answer:
**Topic: The hypocrisy of the preacher (wāʾiz).**

**Ghalib's couplet (maqtā from Ghazal 5):**  
*Transliteration:* Ghalib bura na maan jo waa'iz bura kahe / Aisa bhi koi hai ke sab achha kahein jise[^1]  
*Translation:* Ghalib, don't mind the preacher's bitter way; / Is there a man whom all call good, they say?[^1]

This couplet (17 words) contains **6 Persian-origin words** (bura ×2, na, aisa, koi, sab).[^2]

**Iqbal's couplet (opening of Nazm 1):**  
*Transliteration:* Ajab waa'iz ki deen-daari hai ya Rabb / Adaawat hai use saare jahan se[^3]  
*Translation:* What wondrous piety, O Lord! / He holds the entire world in enmity.[^3]

This couplet (13 words, treating deen-daari as one compound) contains **3 Persian-origin words** (ajab, jahān, dārī).[^4]

Ghalib (roughly 35% Persian-origin words) deploys a denser Persian lexical layer, befitting his intended audience of 19th-century Mughal courtly elites and Delhi literati—readers steeped in Persian poetic traditions who prized linguistic sophistication and esoteric nuance.[^5] Iqbal (roughly 23% Persian-origin words) opts for sparser Persian amid more vernacular Hindustani elements (e.g., hai, use, saare, se, ki), signaling his 20th-century aim at a broader Muslim readership in colonial India: the emerging middle class and masses, to propagate his philosophy of *khudī* (selfhood) accessibly without requiring deep Persian fluency.[^6]

[^1]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, Chapter 5 (“Aaina kyun na doon ke tamasha kahein jise”), [translation/](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/05-aaina-kyun-na-doon-ke-tamasha-kahein-jise/translation/).

[^2]: Classification based on standard Urdu etymology (*platts dictionary* reference for corpus context): Persian loans like *burā* (بد), *na* (نه), *aisā*/*koi*/*sab* (ایسا/کِی/سب, Perso-Urdu core). Arabic (*wāʾiz*) excluded per query's "Persian-origin"; names/Hindi ignored. Full ghazal sustains higher density (e.g., tamashā, nigāh, guldasta).

[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, Chapter 1 (“Ajab waa'iz ki deen-daari hai ya Rabb”), [translation/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/01-ajab-waaiz-ki-deen-daari-hai-ya-rabb/translation/).

[^4]: Persian: *ajab* (عجب), *jahān* (جہان), *dārī* (داری). Arabic (*wāʾiz*, deen, ya, Rabb, adaawat) excluded; Hindi core dominant.

[^5]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, full Chapter 5 [transliteration/](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/05-aaina-kyun-na-doon-ke-tamasha-kahein-jise/transliteration/) shows 50%+ Persian-origin across ghazal (e.g., hasrat, bazm-e-khayāl, afsūn, shagūftan).

[^6]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, full Chapter 1 [transliteration/](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/01-ajab-waaiz-ki-deen-daari-hai-ya-rabb/transliteration/) ~30% Persian-origin, prioritizing rhythmic vernacular for orality/recitation among diverse Muslims.
